perturbation.original_text: This agreement does not cover: Technical information or other information which, at the time when the Inventor/ Disclosing Party provided the Receiving Party with the Information, was considered publicly known or later on becomes publicly known without the Receiving Party being liable for making it publicly known.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement does not cover: Technical secrets or other information which, at the time when the Inventor/ Disclosing Party provided the Receiving Party with the Information, was considered publicly known or later on becomes publicly known without the Receiving Party being liable for making it publicly known.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Technical information' with 'Technical secrets' introduces an in-text contradiction. It is not clear if information and secrets are the same thing in the context of the agreement. If its not the same, then the 'Receiving Party' will assume only the secrets have to be protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement regarding confidential information (hereinafter "Information"), which the Receiving Party has received or will receive from the Inventor/Disclosing Party, is entered in between (hereinafter referred to as the "Inventor/Disclosing Party ") and (hereinafter referred to as the "Receiving Party ") The Information is supplied in the form of the following documents, prototypes or the like: (List of disclosed material): The Receiving Party undertakes to keep the Information confidential and not to disclose it to any third parties and to avoid the risk of the information being disclosed to any third parties.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Technical information' to 'Technical secrets' introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of protection. The original text protected 'Technical information,' while the changed text protects 'Technical secrets.' The 'Contradicted Text' refers to 'confidential information (hereinafter "Information")', which the Receiving Party undertakes to keep confidential. By narrowing the scope to only 'Technical secrets,' the changed text contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which covers a broader category of 'Information,' thus creating a conflict and uncertainty about what must be protected. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, where using terms interchangeably without defining them leads to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party is held liable to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for damages in the event of breach of this Agreement. The Receiving Party is also held liable in case of breach of the Agreement by third parties to whom the Receiving Party has handed over the Information wholly or partly.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party is responsible to the Inventor/Disclosing Party for damages in the event of violation of this Agreement. The Receiving Party is also responsible in case of violation of the Agreement by external entities to whom the Receiving Party has handed over the Information wholly or partly.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'held liable' to 'responsible' and 'breach' to 'violation' and 'third parties' to 'external entities' introduces ambiguity. While the terms may sound similar, they have different legal implications. 'Responsible' may imply a lesser degree of accountability than 'liable.' The same with 'violation' and 'breach'. The same with external entities, by changing it this way, the Receiving Party, won't be sure if they're talking about the same thing with 'third parties'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party undertakes not to reveal the Information to any person with whom he cooperates, to any of his employees or the like, in a way exceeding what is considered necessary or justiﬁable. If considered necessary or justiﬁable, the Receiving Party shall, before passing on the information to third parties, instruct such third parties about the conﬁdentiality of the Information, and they shall sign a conﬁdentiality and non-disclosure agreement. Before the Information can be handed over , wholly or partly, to third parties not mentioned in the above provision, the Receiving Party shall obtain Inventor’s/Disclosing Party’s written consent.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'held liable' to 'responsible' and 'breach' to 'violation' and 'third parties' to 'external entities' introduces ambiguity regarding the level of accountability and the specific parties involved. The contradicted text specifies obligations related to 'third parties' and requires them to sign a confidentiality agreement. The change to 'external entities' creates uncertainty about whether the same obligations apply, potentially weakening the confidentiality requirements outlined in the contradicted text. This ambiguity leads to a strong contradiction because it's unclear whether the receiving party has the same obligation to obtain consent and ensure confidentiality from 'external entities' as it does from 'third parties,' which could affect the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall register all employees or others having gained insight into the Information. The register must be accessible to the Inventor/Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall document all personnel or others having gained access to the Data. The documentation must be accessible to the Inventor/Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'register all employees' to 'document all personnel,' 'gained insight into the Information' to 'gained access to the Data,' and 'The register' to 'The documentation' introduces inconsistency and ambiguity. 'Personnel' may have a different meaning or scope than 'employees,' creating in-text contradictions of who must be documented. Furthermore, it creates inconsistencies if the Data is a reference to the information that needs to be protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party undertakes not to reveal the Information to any person with whom he cooperates, to any of his employees or the like, in a way exceeding what is considered necessary or justiﬁable. If considered necessary or justiﬁable, the Receiving Party shall, before passing on the information to third parties, instruct such third parties about the conﬁdentiality of the Information, and they shall sign a conﬁdentiality and non-disclosure agreement. Before the Information can be handed over , wholly or partly, to third parties not mentioned in the above provision, the Receiving Party shall obtain Inventor’s/Disclosing Party’s written consent.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'register all employees' to 'document all personnel' creates ambiguity. 'Personnel' may include individuals beyond 'employees,' potentially conflicting with the contradicted text, which specifies 'employees or the like.' The contradicted text discusses revealing 'the Information to any person with whom he cooperates, to any of his employees or the like' implying a specific group. The changed text's broader term 'personnel' creates an inconsistency in who needs to be documented and under what confidentiality obligations, fitting the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition, which states that using multiple terms interchangeably without defining them can lead to conflicting obligations. The change also from 'gained insight into the Information' to 'gained access to the Data' and 'register' to 'document' makes the two texts incompatible.
